flashcards.back: Programmed I/O is a method where the CPU continuously polls a device's status register to check if it is ready for data transfer. The CPU executes a loop, reading the status bit until the device is ready, then reads/writes data. This approach is simple but wastes CPU cycles waiting.

Interrupt-driven I/O allows the device to signal the CPU via an interrupt request (IRQ) when it is ready for data transfer, freeing the CPU from polling. The CPU only spends time handling the interrupt, improving overall system efficiency compared to programmed I/O.

DMA is a technique that allows peripheral devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, using a DMA controller. The CPU sets up the transfer parameters, then the DMA controller handles the data movement, offloading the CPU for the duration of the transfer.

flashcards.front: Compare Programmed I/O and Interrupt-driven I/O in terms of CPU usage.
flashcards.back: In Programmed I/O, the CPU is fully occupied waiting for the device, while in Interrupt-driven I/O the CPU performs other tasks until an interrupt signals readiness, reducing idle waiting time. Interrupt-driven I/O thus yields higher CPU utilization and better multitasking.

flashcards.front: Compare Interrupt-driven I/O and DMA regarding throughput and CPU involvement.
flashcards.back: Interrupt-driven I/O still requires the CPU to copy each data word between the device and memory, limiting transfer speed; DMA can move blocks of data autonomously, achieving higher throughput and further reducing CPU overhead. DMA is preferred for high-bandwidth devices like hard disks and network cards.

flashcards.front: List one advantage and one disadvantage of Programmed I/O.
flashcards.back: Advantage: Simple to implement and requires no additional hardware beyond the device status register. Disadvantage: Inefficient due to CPU busy-waiting, especially for slow devices, leading to poor utilization of processor resources.

flashcards.front: List one advantage and one disadvantage of DMA.
flashcards.back: Advantage: High-speed data transfers that free the CPU, enabling concurrent processing. Disadvantage: DMA controllers add hardware cost and complexity, and may require careful handling of memory protection and cache coherence.

flashcards.front: Give a practical example of DMA usage in a computer system.
flashcards.back: When reading a large file from a hard disk, the DMA controller transfers the data blocks directly into main memory without CPU intervention, allowing the CPU to execute other instructions while the disk I/O proceeds, resulting in faster overall system performance.
